mnemonics.title: D.C.D. - Data Couples Draw Communication
mnemonics.content: Data Couples (shown as arrows with data labels) Draw Communication between modules. Remember: Solid arrow with data label = Data Couple.

mnemonics.title: C.F.S. - Control Flags Show Status
mnemonics.content: Control Flags (shown as dashed arrows) Show Status information passed between modules. Remember: Dashed line without data = Control Flag.

mnemonics.title: T.O.P. - Top Of Pyramid Structure
mnemonics.content: Structure Charts follow a Top Of Pyramid approach - the main module at Level 0 calls Level 1 modules, which call Level 2 modules, and so on.

mnemonics.title: T.A.N. - Transaction Analysis Note
mnemonics.content: Transaction centers Analyze and Note the type of transaction, then route to appropriate subordinate module (shown as a double-lined arrow).

mnemonics.title: M.A.P. - Modules Are Parents
mnemonics.content: Modules that call other modules are called Parents (superordinate), and modules called by others are Children (subordinate). Parents are drawn above Children.

acronyms.usage: A diagram showing the hierarchy of modules in a system, used in structured design to decompose a system into manageable modules.
Data passed from one module to another. Represented by a solid arrow with data name. Used when actual data values are transmitted.
Information passed to indicate status or decision. Represented by a dashed arrow without data name. Used for switches, flags, or boolean indicators.
A module that analyzes input and routes to different sub-modules based on transaction type. Look for the diamond symbol in the module.
A module that receives input, transforms it, and produces output. Used in transform-centered design where data flows through processing stages.
While not part of Structure Charts, remember: DFDs show HOW data flows (processes), while Structure Charts show WHO does the work (modules).

visualTips.visualization: Visualize an inverted pyramid or organizational chart. The boss (main module) sits at the top, with employees (subordinate modules) below. Arrows go downward showing who reports to whom.
Think of Data Couple as a DELIVERY TRUCK (solid, carries actual goods/data). Think of Control Flag as a SIGNAL FLARE (dashed, just indicates status/warning). Solid = Data, Dashed = Status.
Imagine a TOLL BOOTH or AIRPORT CHECK-IN. The transaction center (toll booth) checks what type of vehicle/passenger it is, then directs it to the appropriate lane (subordinate module).
Think of an ASSEMBLY LINE. Raw material (input) enters, goes through various processes (transform centers), and emerges as finished product (output). Each station adds value.

Remember: LOW COUPLING (few connections between modules) = GOOD design. Think of it like good neighbors - they communicate but don't depend on each other. High cohesion = each module does ONE thing well.

analogies.analogy: Like a FAMILY TREE or CORPORATE ORGANIZATIONAL CHART. The CEO (main module) is at the top, department heads (Level 1) report to CEO, employees (Level 2) report to department heads. Information flows down through commands.
Like a KITCHEN RECIPE PASSING. When a head chef calls a sous chef, they pass actual ingredients (Data Couple) - flour, eggs, milk - that the sous chef will use to make something.
Like a FLIGHT STATUS BOARD. The board doesn't pass actual passengers to gates; it just indicates status - 'ON TIME', 'DELAYED', 'BOARDING' - helping gates decide what to do.
Like a BANK TELLER or CUSTOMER SERVICE DESK. When you walk in, the teller checks what you need (withdrawal, deposit, inquiry) and routes you to the appropriate window/assistant.

Like a LAUNDRY WASHING MACHINE. It takes dirty clothes (input), performs transformation (wash, spin), and produces clean clothes (output). The machine doesn't route to different places - it just processes.

Think of MODULES as INDEPENDENT CONTRACTORS. Each has a specific job, communicates through clear contracts (data couples), and doesn't need to know how other contractors do their work. This makes the system easy to maintain and modify.
